I've found comprehensive search results about bento grid layout CSS implementation with featured and hero cards in asymmetric grid design systems. Here's what the sources reveal:

## Core Concepts

The Bento Grid takes its name from Japanese bento boxes, those compartmentalized meals where each element occupies a defined but harmonious space. Apple popularized this layout style at its keynotes, presenting product features in visually striking asymmetric grids.

Unlike traditional uniform grids, the Bento Grid plays on size contrasts: wide cards sit alongside compact cards, creating a natural visual hierarchy that guides the user's eye towards important elements.

## CSS Implementation

A basic implementation uses `.bento-grid { display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(4, 1fr); grid-template-rows: repeat(3, 150px); gap: 16px; }` with large cards using `grid-column: span 2; grid-row: span 2;`

The pattern provides a practical, component-driven approach to building asymmetric "bento" card grids using simple HTML and utility CSS (Tailwind) patterns.

## Design Hierarchy

To create effective Bento Grids, keep these principles in mind: A single "hero" card: the largest, it carries the main message · 2-3 medium cards: the important secondary features · Multiple small cards: details and complementary information

Ready-made card variants include Hero (2x2), Standard, Accent, and full-width CTA—plus responsive grid configurations (2-, 3-, 4-column)

## Use Cases & Benefits

Apple's implementation features a dominant hero card showing the product itself, surrounded by smaller cards highlighting individual features with icons and brief descriptions. The size hierarchy directly maps to information importance: the product visualization receives most attention, while supporting details are accessible but secondary.

Tasks are completed 23% faster with modular layouts compared to traditional linear designs.

The search results provide extensive CSS code examples, responsive design patterns, and production-ready implementations using both vanilla CSS Grid and Tailwind CSS utilities.
